name: Vaughn Monroe
abstract: Vaughn Wilton Monroe (October 7, 1911 – May 21, 1973) was an American baritone singer, trumpeter and big band leader who was most popular in the 1940s and 1950s. He has two stars on the Hollywood Walk of Fame, one for recording and another for radio performance.

about_quotes.speaker: Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: So I jumped on a bus and played Binghamton... went back to school the following Monday... I went on the road with Vaughn, and we played about ten weeks of theaters
about_quotes.doc_title: Bucky Pizzarelli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, May 23, 2003 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Bucky Pizzarelli
about_quotes.evidence: Was Vaughn Monroe your first full time— Oh yeah. I was just 17, I just got out of high school...
about_quotes.doc_title: Bucky Pizzarelli interviewed by Monk Rowe, Aspen, Colorado, October 11, 1997 [interview 1 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Sonny Igoe
about_quotes.evidence: It was all jazz and big bands, dance bands, you know like Vaughn Monroe, Jimmy Dorsey, Tommy Dorsey, Benny Goodman. They were jazz players but they were primarily dance bands.
about_quotes.doc_title: Sonny Igoe interviewed by Monk Rowe, Emerson, New Jersey, July 31, 2003

about_quotes.speaker: Phil Woods
about_quotes.evidence: Whether you were with Guy Lombardo or Vaughan Monroe, the same guys could go with Woody Herman...
about_quotes.doc_title: Phil Woods interviewed by Monk Rowe, Delaware Water Gap, PA, November 8, 1999

about_quotes.speaker: Bobby Johnson Jr.
about_quotes.evidence: Next thing I knew Vaughn Monroe— [INTERVIEWER] Picked it up and did a cover of it or something.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bobby Johnson Jr. interviewed by Monk Rowe, Ellenville, New York, June 23, 1998

about_quotes.speaker: Ray Shiner
about_quotes.evidence: when we did the Camel Caravan with the — if you’ll pardon the expression — Vaughan Monroe.
about_quotes.doc_title: Ray Shiner interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 11, 1998
